findings.summary: Refusal/error phrases are defined in three separate, partially overlapping locations with no single source of truth
evidence: config.py lines 113-119: default_ocr_refusal_markers defines 5 phrases used by OCR provider at runtime
classifier/constants.py lines 62-68: ERROR_PHRASES tuple defines 5 phrases (partially overlapping) used by classifier quality_gates
classifier/prompts.py lines 33-35: hardcodes 3 error-detection phrases in the LLM system prompt text
OCR provider uses settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS while classifier uses constants.ERROR_PHRASES - these lists partially overlap but diverge
findings.suggestion: Consolidate refusal phrases into a single source (e.g. a shared constant in common/) and derive the config defaults and classifier constants from it.

findings.summary: Inconsistent exception catching: some call sites catch specific exceptions while parallel call sites use bare 'except Exception'
evidence: tags.py get_latest_tags line 79 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError) for get_document()
claims.py claim_processing_tag lines 52, 77, 87 catch bare Exception for the same get_document() and update_document_metadata() calls
stale_lock.py lines 51, 78 catch bare Exception for get_documents_by_tag() and update_document_metadata()
ocr/worker.py _refresh_document line 155 catches bare Exception for get_document()
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a consistent exception set for PaperlessClient calls across all call sites.

findings.summary: Tag lifecycle operations silently swallow exceptions without propagation or recovery, making failures invisible to callers
evidence: tags.py remove_stale_queue_tag lines 114-121: catches bare Exception, logs, but does not propagate or return failure status
tags.py release_processing_tag lines 152-172: catches bare Exception in two places, logs, returns None regardless
stale_lock.py recover_stale_locks lines 78-83: catches bare Exception per doc, logs, continues silently
findings.suggestion: For release_processing_tag and remove_stale_queue_tag, return a boolean indicating success/failure so callers can take corrective action.

findings.summary: finalize_document_with_error accepts a logger instance as a parameter, breaking the convention that modules use their own module-level logger
evidence: tags.py finalize_document_with_error line 184: takes 'log: structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger' as a parameter
ocr/worker.py line 275: passes its module-level log to finalize_document_with_error
classifier/worker.py line 239: passes its module-level log to finalize_document_with_error
Every other function in the codebase uses module-level 'log = structlog.get_logger(__name__)' without parameter passing
findings.suggestion: Remove the log parameter from finalize_document_with_error and use a module-level logger instead.

findings.summary: library_setup.py mutates the openai module's global state (base_url, api_key, http_client), creating implicit coupling
evidence: library_setup.py lines 57-62: sets openai.base_url, openai.api_key, openai.http_client as module-level globals
llm.py line 35: OpenAIChatMixin._create_completion calls openai.chat.completions.create() relying on this implicit global state
findings.suggestion: Create an explicit openai.Client instance in library_setup and pass it through Settings or a dedicated holder.

findings.summary: OCR and classifier providers track stats with the same pattern but different keys, reset behavior, and no shared abstraction
evidence: ocr/provider.py lines 99-115: initializes _stats in __init__ with 4 keys, never resets
classifier/provider.py lines 52-68: initializes _stats via _reset_stats() with 7 keys, resets on each classify_text call
Both use identical _inc_stat() and get_stats() method patterns with threading.Lock
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared ProviderStats helper class with configurable keys and optional reset.

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon uses string attribute names to look up tag IDs from Settings, bypassing type checking
evidence: bootstrap.py line 73: getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr)
ocr/daemon.py lines 77-78: passes string literals 'OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' and 'PRE_TAG_ID'
classifier/daemon.py lines 54-55: passes string literals 'CLASSIFY_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' and 'CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID'
findings.suggestion: Accept the tag IDs directly as int|None parameters instead of string attribute names.

findings.summary: Both daemons create a new PaperlessClient per document, discarding connection reuse benefits
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 44: 'paperless = PaperlessClient(settings)' inside _process_document
classifier/daemon.py line 77: 'paperless = PaperlessClient(settings)' inside process_document closure
findings.suggestion: Use a shared PaperlessClient per worker thread rather than creating and destroying a client per document.

findings.summary: pyproject.toml pins httpx~=0.27 but installed version is 0.28
evidence: pyproject.toml line: 'httpx~=0.27'
pip list shows 'httpx 0.28.1' installed
The ~= compatible release operator means >=0.27, <0.28, so 0.28.1 violates the constraint
findings.suggestion: Update the httpx pin to ~=0.28 to match the actually running version.

findings.summary: OCR worker catches RuntimeError specifically from bytes_to_images but lets all other exceptions propagate unhandled
evidence: ocr/worker.py line 96: catches RuntimeError specifically from bytes_to_images
classifier/worker.py: no top-level try/except in process()
daemon_loop.py line 55: catches as 'Work item failed' but document left in claimed state
findings.suggestion: Add a top-level try/except in both workers' process() methods that catches unexpected exceptions and calls _finalize_with_error.

findings.summary: Module-level and method docstrings frequently restate what the code already makes obvious
evidence: paperless.py line 5-12: docstring restates class name and methods
concurrency.py line 52: docstring 'Context manager that acquires the LLM concurrency semaphore' restates method name
claims.py lines 34-45: docstring repeats the 4-step workflow visible in the function body
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings to only document non-obvious behavior, edge cases, or domain reasoning.

findings.summary: RST-style section headers with === underlines in module docstrings are uniformly applied, a hallmark of LLM-generated documentation
evidence: Every module docstring follows the exact same pattern: title on line 2, followed by '=====' underline
This uniformity across 20+ modules is unnatural for human-written code
findings.suggestion: Simplify module docstrings to a single descriptive sentence or short paragraph. Remove RST-style headers.

findings.summary: Unreachable code paths with defensive RuntimeError raises that add no value
evidence: retry.py line 98: 'raise RuntimeError("unreachable...")' -- truly unreachable
paperless.py lines 129-133: 'raise RuntimeError(... "no candidates tried")' with pragma: no cover
findings.suggestion: Remove unreachable RuntimeError guards.

findings.summary: Step-by-step numbered comments that restate code intent already clear from method names
evidence: claims.py lines 49-98: '# Step 1: Refresh', '# Step 2: Check', '# Step 3: Patch', '# Step 4: Verify'
findings.suggestion: Remove numbered step comments. The code is self-documenting.

findings.summary: common/__init__.py is a long hand-written catalog of every module in prose
evidence: __init__.py lines 1-21: 21-line docstring that enumerates every module
findings.suggestion: Replace with a one-line package purpose statement.

findings.summary: Multiple modules include 'Typical usage::' code examples that restate obvious import paths
evidence: retry.py lines 17-27: 'Typical usage::' example
concurrency.py lines 9-16: 'Typical usage::' example
These are LLM-hallmark patterns for internal modules with one or two call sites
findings.suggestion: Remove 'Typical usage' blocks from internal modules.

findings.summary: Module-level wrapper functions init_llm_semaphore() and llm_semaphore() merely forward to the llm_limiter singleton
evidence: init_llm_semaphore() is a one-line wrapper: llm_limiter.init(max_concurrent)
llm_semaphore() is a one-line wrapper: with llm_limiter.acquire(): yield
findings.suggestion: Remove the free-function wrappers and have callers use llm_limiter directly.

findings.summary: Classifier daemon docstring references wrong module path for iter helper
evidence: _iter_docs_to_classify docstring says 'Delegates to the shared :func:`~common.tags.iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag`' but the actual import is from common.document_iter
findings.suggestion: Fix the docstring to reference common.document_iter.iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider uses _reset_stats() method while OpenAIProvider initializes stats dict in __init__
evidence: ClassificationProvider._reset_stats() reinitializes self._stats dict on each classify_text call; OpenAIProvider initializes stats once in __init__ and accumulates across calls
findings.suggestion: Align stats lifecycle: either both reset per-call or both accumulate.

findings.summary: OCR daemon uses module-level _process_document function; classifier daemon uses inline closure in main()
evidence: ocr/daemon.py: 'def _process_document(doc: dict, settings: Settings) -> None:' at module level; classifier/daemon.py: 'def process_document(doc: dict) -> None:' as closure inside main()
findings.suggestion: Use the same pattern in both daemons.

findings.summary: Both src/ocr/prompts.py and src/classifier/prompts.py lack test files
evidence: No test_prompts.py exists in tests/unit/ocr/ or tests/unit/classifier/
findings.suggestion: Add schema validation test for CLASSIFICATION_JSON_SCHEMA ensuring required fields match ClassificationResult fields.

findings.summary: OCR worker uses f-string in log message argument instead of structured logging field
evidence: Line 233: 'log.warning(f"OCR produced {reason}; marking error", doc_id=self.doc_id)' — uses f-string interpolation in event message
findings.suggestion: Change to log.warning('OCR produced error content; marking error', doc_id=self.doc_id, reason=reason)
